coredata.dc:title: Phosphorus-doped carbon dots for sensing both Au (III) and l-methionine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Zhangbao
Wang, Shanshan
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Herein, one type of carbon dots (CDs) directed by l-threonine has been innovatively synthesized on the basis of a facile approach. Meanwhile, the CDs mainly consisted of C, O, N, and P according to the detailed characterizations. Importantly, the CDs obtained here not only exhibited the bright-blue fluorescence and generally acceptable stability, but also showed the promising for detecting Au3+ and l-methionine. To be specific, the off-process by an obvious fluorescence quenching of the CDs achieved assaying Au3+, and Au3+ could be removed by the introduction of l-methionine through the stronger interaction between Au3+ and l-methionine, and thus leading to the fluorescence of CDs recovered. Consequently, the quantitative analyses of Au3+ concentration in a linear range of 6 μM–1 mM was obtained along with the range from 80 μM to 1 mM for l-methionine. In addition, the practicability of this proposed method was validated by the detections of the lake samples, suggesting that it could broaden the sensing strategies for assaying both Au3+ and l-methionine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Detection
Au3+
l-Methionine

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CDs by Au3+
Optimizing the synthesis conditions of CDs
Principle of detecting Au3+ and l-methionine
Building up the assay for Au3+ and l-methionine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesizing CDs and sensing both Au3+ and l-methionine.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs, Inset: photographs of CDs with daylight (a) and UV light (b); (B) Emission spectra of CDs for varying excitation wavelengths; (C) HR-TEM image and DLS analysis of CDs; (D) FTIR spectrum for these CDs.
The fluorescence stability of CDs along with different temperature (A), concentrations of NaCl (B), time (C) and organic solvents (D).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs in the presence Au3+, CDs with both Au3+ and l-methionine; (B) Size distributions of CDs, CDs by adding Au3+, CDs with both Au3+ and l-methionine; (C) Response of CDs to Au3+ (1 mM) and other metal ions (1 mM); (D) Response of CDs-Au3+ to l-methionine and other substances (1 mM).

Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of varied concentrations of Au3+ (A), and l-methionine (C); Plot of the change of fluorescence intensity versus the logarithmic concentrations of Au3+ (B), and l-methionine (D) added.
Recoveries of the lake sample supplemented with Au3+ by the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Phosphorus-doped carbon dots for sensing both Au (III) and l-methionine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Ministry of Education), Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Ministry of Education)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, one kind of carbon dots (CDs) from l-threonine has been successfully synthesized by a facile way in the aqueous solution. Importantly, the fluorescence of CDs could be obviously quenched once Au3+ was introduced, owing to the complexes of Au3+ and CDs formed. Moreover, the interaction between CDs and Au3+ could be replaced by that of the added l-methionine with Au3+, thus leading to the quenched fluorescence of this prepared CDs recovered.

Herein, one type of carbon dots (CDs) directed by l-threonine has been innovatively synthesized on the basis of a facile approach. Meanwhile, the CDs mainly consisted of C, O, N, and P according to the detailed characterizations. Importantly, the CDs obtained here not only exhibited the bright-blue fluorescence and generally acceptable stability, but also showed the promising for detecting Au3+ and l-methionine. To be specific, the off-process by an obvious fluorescence quenching of the CDs achieved assaying Au3+, and Au3+ could be removed by the introduction of l-methionine through the stronger interaction between Au3+ and l-methionine, and thus leading to the fluorescence of CDs recovered. Consequently, the quantitative analyses of Au3+ concentration in a linear range of 6 μM–1 mM was obtained along with the range from 80 μM to 1 mM for l-methionine. In addition, the practicability of this proposed method was validated by the detections of the lake samples, suggesting that it could broaden the sensing strategies for assaying both Au3+ and l-methionine.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), one critical member of environmental nanomaterial with the distinct fluorescence, have attracted the special attention over the past decade [1,2]. Hence, various methods have been raised for synthesizing the fluorescent CDs. Basically, there have emerged two major routes for elucidating the techniques of preparing CDs [3–5]. The first way is known as top-down, mainly consisting of electrochemical oxidation [6,7], arc discharge [8], acid/sulfuric acid oxidation [9,10] and laser ablation [11]. Bottom-up serves as another one, such as hydrothermal [12], microwave [13] and ultrasonic [14]. Doping CDs with heteroatomic (such as nitrogen, phosphorus, boron) have attracted the widespread attentions due to their obvious advantages of changing the electronic structures of CDs, tuning their chemical and optical properties, which therefore extends the potential applications of CDs [15–17].

As well as their favorable fluorescence, CDs indeed show a series of advantages including the expected quantum-yield, water solubility, fantastic catalytic performance and biocompatibility [18–20]. Accordingly, these properties promote CDs to be widely employed in kinds of fields such as biosensing, biolabeling, catalysis and beyond [21–24]. Especially, CDs are frequently designed and developed as the sensors towards various detecting applications while their fluorescence variations of CDs serve as the sensing signals, such as several metal ions and biological-active molecule [25–32]. However, the detections of Au3+ were few reported by quenching the fluorescence of CDs, and assaying Au3+ is critically important since Au3+ in the aqueous solution definitely induces the toxicity of the living system [33,34] Meanwhile, l-methionine serves as one of the essential amino acids and has the interaction with Au3+, and its corresponding detections may be accomplished by virtue of Au3+.

Herein, we have innovatively established a facile method of synthesizing CDs, while l-threonine played the role as the carbon source. Likewise, the as-prepared CDs obviously showed the blue fluorescence, superior dispersity and acceptable stability in aqueous solution. Importantly, we developed a fluorimetric assay for monitoring the levels of both Au3+ and l-methionine based on the variations of CDs. To be specific, it was proved that the fluorescence of CDs could be obviously quenched by Au3+ owing to the adsorption of Au3+ to the surface of CDs, and the further introduction of l-methionine could lead to the fluorescence recovering, which was caused by the stronger interaction between Au3+ and l-methionine. Consequently, the simple on-off-on strategy applied for sensing both Au3+ and l-methionine has been successfully proposed (Fig. 1 ).

l-Threonine, albumin from bovine serum (BSA), glutathione (GSH), pepsin, cysteine, l-methionine, urea, glycine, arginine, threonine, thiamine, nicotinic acid and penicillin were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate (HAuCl4), sucrose, lactose, glucose were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, U.S.). All the ions including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sr2+, I−, K+, phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5), glacial acetic acid (HAc), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), boric acid (H3BO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (20 mM, pH 2.3–12) were prepared. Ultrapure water, 18.25 MΩ cm, produced using an Aquapro AWL-0502-Pultrapure water system (Chongqing, China) was employed during the whole process of the experiments.

All fluorescence measurements were finished on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) with an excitation slit set at 10 nm band pass and emission at 5 nm band pass in 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell. Meanwhile, UV/vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Again, the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained by using a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Portland, America) at 200 KV. Also, the size distribution data was acquired via a Zetasizer Nano Dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (Malvern, England). Furthermore, elemental and functional group analyses were identified by ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. A Fangzhong pHS-3C digital pH meter (Chengdu, China) was applied to measure the pH values of the aqueous solutions, and a vortex mixer QL-901 (Haimen, China) was employed for blending the solutions. The thermostatic water bath (DF-101s) was purchased from Gongyi Instrument Co., Ltd (Gongyi, China). Finally, the powder of CDs was obtained by lyophilization with PiloFD8-4.3V (Charlotte, USA).

All the glassware for the experiments were thoroughly cleaned by freshly prepared aqua regia followed by rinsed with the distilled water, and further subjected to oven-dried. In brief, the carbon dots with blue emission were rapidly produced by mixing l-threonine, P2O5 and water. Specifically, 0.1 g of l-threonine was initially mixed with 2 g of P2O5, and 2 mL of water was subsequently quickly added to the mixture. Then, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min and dialyzed by 100 MWCO membrane, and the purified CDs was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the larger product. Finally, the powder of CDs was obtained by lyophilisation and dissolved in ultrapure water with the concentration of 2 mg/mL, and these products were stored at 4 °C before further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CDs by Au3+
ce:section.ce:para: Towards sensing Au3+, 800 μL of Milli-Q purified water and 100 μL of CDs (2 mg/mL) for each were successively pipetted into 1.5 mL of vials, and a series of 100 μL Au3+ solutions with different concentrations (0.06, 0.08, 0.2, 0.6, 1, 2, 4, 6, 10 mM/L) were separately added into the previous vials. Then, the mixtures were incubated for 15 min in the 30 °C water-bath, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra of CDs were subsequently recorded, respectively. To evaluate the selectivity for assaying Au3+, other metal ions including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+ Co2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sr2+ (1 mM/L for each) were introduced into the CDs, respectively.

Firstly, 700 μL of Milli-Q purified water, 100 μL of CDs (2 mg/mL) and Au3+ (100 μL, 10 μM) were mixed into 1.5 mL of vials, and the mixtures were subjected to the incubation of 20 min in the 30 °C water-bath. Next, 100 μL of l-methionine solutions with various concentrations (0.8, 1, 2, 4, 7, 9, and 10 mM/L) were added into the previous mixtures. Finally, all the above solutions were incubated to react at 30 °C for 90 min and their corresponding fluorescence spectra of CDs were recorded. To examine the selectivity for sensing l-methionine, cysteine, glycine, arginine, threonine, BSA, GSH, pepsin, thiamine, nicotinic acid, penicillin (1 mM/L for each) were separately introduced to the system of CDs-Au3+ upon the optimal conditions.

Basically, CDs was innovatively synthesized here on the basis of a facile approach, while the l-threonine mainly functioned as the carbon source. Subsequently, the current CDs were subjected to the characterization by fluorescence spectroscopy. Particularly, the maximum emission appeared at 410 nm originated from an excitation wavelength of 330 nm (Fig. 2 A), whereas l-threonine or P2O5 exhibited no fluorescence in the range of 350 nm–600 nm (Fig. S1). Meanwhile, the aqueous solution of CDs appeared as transparently yellow under the daylight (Fig. 2A, photograph a), while obviously emitted the blue fluorescence (Fig. 1A, photograph b) under UV light (365 nm), describing their promising fluorescence. Simultaneously, two peaks at 280 nm and 330 nm of the absorption spectrum for CDs have been recorded (Fig. S2). Moreover, the emission peak shifted to the longer wavelengths as well as the fluorescence intensity decreased with the excitation wavelengths varying from 330 nm to 390 nm (Fig. 2B), suggesting the emissions of the CDs were excitation-dependent. To visualize CDs prepared here, HR-TEM was employed, and the obtained images revealed that the majority of CDs were well-dispersed. Likewise, their sizes were further confirmed as 0.5–1.2 nm and their average diameter was about 0.62 nm by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. 2C). Besides, the quantum yield of CDs was determined as1.3% by Quantaurus-QY.

Next, the functional groups of the as-synthesized CDs were identified by FTIR. Briefly, the peak at 3410 cm−1 (Fig. 2D) was ascribed to the characteristic absorption of OH stretching vibration, while the CO stretching vibration peaks located at 1630 cm−1. Similarly, the peaks at 1010 cm−1 were attributed to CO stretching vibration. All the above analyses indicated the existence of both carboxylic groups and hydroxyl groups for CDs [35,36]. Besides, XPS survey spectra were acquired towards elucidating the components of the proposed CDs. Accordingly, there emerged four peaks at 141.5, 295.3, 409.1, and 540.2 eV (attributed to P 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s respectively) of the spectrum (Fig. S3A), demonstrating that these CDs were mainly composed of P (13.44%), C (28.23%), O (55.75%) and N (2.16%). Likewise, there were four peaks displayed at 284.6 eV, 285.0 eV, 286.5 eV and 289.2 eV of the C 1s spectrum (Fig. S3B), showing the existence of CC, CN/CO, COC and COOH on the surfaces of CDs. Again, the three peaks referred to 531.6 eV (COC), 532.2 eV (CO) and 533.25 eV (COH) of the O1s spectrum (Fig. S3C) [37,38]. Taken FTIR and XPS spectra together, these date suggested that CDs were equipped with the characteristic functional groups mainly including OH and COOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the synthesis conditions of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the experiment was designed and performed to explore the optimal conditions towards synthesizing CDs. In detail, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs exhibited the obvious change along with varying amounts of P2O5. Different amounts of P2O5 were applied during the synthesis process to obtain the maximum fluorescence intensity of CDs. Finally, 2 g of P2O5, 0.1 g of l-threonine and 2 ml H2O was chosen as the optimized amount (Fig. S4).

To evaluate the capability of CDs for bearing the circumstances, the corresponding experiments were performed. In general, the current CDs exhibited being stable with varying temperatures (25 °C, 30 °C, 35 °C, 40 °C, 45 °C, 50 °C, 55 °C, 60 °C, 65 °C, 70 °C,75 °C) (Fig. 3 A), while it showed the slight alteration accompanied by different concentrations of NaCl (0.1–1 M), (Fig. 3B). Similarly, the fluorescent intensities of CDs showed scarce variation under UV light for different time (Fig. 3C). In addition, the CDs exhibited different fluorescence intensities while existing in different organic solvents (Fig. 3D). Overall, the detailed exploration above suggests their satisfactory stability of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of detecting Au3+ and l-methionine
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the superior fluorescent properties of the CDs, we addressed whether they can be used as the fluorescence probes. In particular, the fluorescence intensity at 410 nm decreased upon introducing Au3+ with the concentration of 1 mM. Interestingly, the fluorescence of CDs dramatically recovered by adding 1 mM l-methionine (Fig. 4 A), demonstrating that the CDs showed the possibility for sensing Au3+ and l-methionine base on the on-off-on process. Simultaneously, the related variations of the fluorescence and colour for CDs were recorded with both daylight and UV light. Also, the color of the CDs solution changed from transparently yellow to gray in the presence of Au3+ under the daylight, while varying as yellow-brown with introducing l-methionine (Fig. 4B). Towards the mechanism of sensing Au3+, the aggregations possibly caused the fluorescence quenching by adding gold ions. As shown in Fig. 4B, the related particle size of the CDs varied from 0.62 ± 0.4 nm to 21.1 ± 0.8 nm by 1 mM Au3+. These results indicated that the obvious aggregation of CDs occurred when gold ions were introduced, thus contributed to the fluorescence quenching of CDs. Additionally, l-methionine, as one of the thiol-containing amino acids, shows the interaction with Au3+, it could preferentially interact with gold ions, and thus removing gold ions from the complex of CDs-Au3+, because there existed the stronger affinity between l-methionine and gold ions. Accordingly, the complex of l-methionine and Au3+ replaced that of CDs with Au3+, leading to their fluorescence recovery of CDs. Likewise, their sizes returned to 0.71 ± 0.5 nm by introducing l-methionine [39–42].

The fluorescence intensities exhibited little change by introducing Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sr2+ (10−2 M/L for each), describing the favorable selectivity of CDs for sensing Au3+ (Fig. 4C). Also, the possible effect originated from coexisting substance on measuring Au3+ was tested, and the related experiments were designed while Au3+ (1 mM) was in the presence of Na+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, lactose, glucose, glutamic acid, histidine, glycine, arginine, urea, BSA and pepsin (1 mM, 500 μM, 100 μM, 10 μM), respectively. As a result, there existed little variation of the fluorescence signals, proving that coexisting substance showed little effect on the detections of Au3+ (Fig. S5A).

Also, the selectivity of the probe for assaying l-methionine was explored by investigating the responses to other substances (GSH, cysteine, glycine, arginine, threonine, BSA, pepsin, thiamine, nicotinic acid, penicillin with the concentration of 10−3 M). As shown in Fig. 4D, the results described that the complex of CDs-Au3+ could also selectively detect l-methionine. Again, the interference experiments were further conducted for testing other possible effect from lactose, glucose, urea, K+, Na+, I−, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and Ni2+ with the various concentrations of 1 mM, 500 μM, 100 μM, 10 μM, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Au3+-l-methionine scarcely varied (Fig. S5B). Hence, these results suggest that the designed strategy for assaying Au3+ and l-methionine shows being selective with the acceptable endurance for interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Building up the assay for Au3+ and l-methionine
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the optimal sensing performance, the analytical conditions including the incubation time, temperature and pH of CDs detecting Au3+ have been explored. Accordingly, the variation of the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum while the reaction time of 10 min, incubation temperature of 30 °C and incubation pH of 6 (BR) served as the conditions (Fig. S6A–C). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased along with the logarithmic plots of Au3+ concentrations (6, 8, 20, 60, 100, 200, 400, 600, and 1000 μM). Simultaneously, F0–F decrease versus the concentrations displayed a linear range of 6 μM–1 mM with its correlation coefficient of 0.992 (Fig. 5 A and B), and the detection limit was 5.86 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Thus, CDs shows the promising as a probe for sensing Au3+ towards other purpose.

Similarly, the potential effects of the incubation time and temperature on the detections of l-methionine were examined. Through the optimizations, 90 min, 30 °C and pH 6.0 (BR) were selected as the final conditions for the following experiments (Fig. S7). Furthermore, the standard curve of this sensing model was built up by introducing various concentrations of l-methionine (1 mM/L, 900 μM/L, 700 μM/L, 400 μM/L, 200 μM/L, 100 μM/L, 80 μM/L). The F–F0 of CDs regularly decreased with the concentrations of l-methionine varying from 80 μM/L to 1 mM/L, resulting in a typical dose-response curve together with its correlation coefficient of 0.977 (Fig. 5C and D), and the detection limit was 80 μM/L. Therefore, these results demonstrate that the CDs-Au3+ complex provided a possible way for assaying l-methionine. Moreover, the proposed method were compared with other sensors as Table S1, describing that the current CDs exhibited the acceptable linear range.

Generally, the as-synthesized CDs showed the satisfactory stability in the real samples. In order to clarify the practical application of the proposed method, the standard recovery experiments were performed by employing lake sample adding other metal ion including Ca2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+ at the concentration of 100 μM (Table 1 ), suggesting the current method may broaden ways for practically detecting Au3+ in the real samples.

On the whole, we have successfully proposed a facile strategy to synthesize the water-soluble fluorescent CDs as well as l-threonine serving as the carbon source. Specifically, the fluorescence of CDs could be obviously quenched once Au3+ was introduced, owing to the complexes of Au3+ and CDs formed. Importantly, the interaction between CDs and Au3+ could be replaced by that of the added l-methionine with Au3+, thus leading to the quenched fluorescence of this prepared CDs recovered. In addition, these CDs on the basis of on-off-on process for detecting Au3+ and l-methionine showed the favorable selectivity and acceptable practicability. Finally, this study of the proposed CDs broadens the avenues for sensing both Au3+ and l-methionine.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financialsupport by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2018AC004), Fundamental Research Funds for Innovative Project on Designing and Screening Drug Candidates of Chongqing (cstc2015zdcy-ztzx120003), Chongqing Research Project of Basic Research and Frontier Exploration (cstc2018jcyjAX0197).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2018.08.001.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
